    27→open scoped EuclideanGeometry
    28→
    29→/-- Oriented angles make sense in 2d.
    30→
    31→Note: this can't blindly be added to mathlib as it creates an "instance diamond"
    32→with an instance for modules satisfying `is_empty`. -/
    33→noncomputable instance Module.orientedEuclideanSpaceFinTwo : Module.Oriented ℝ ℝ² (Fin 2) :=
    34→  ⟨Basis.orientation <| PiLp.basisFun 2 _ _⟩
    35→
    36→/-- Two dimensional euclidean space is two-dimensional. -/
    37→instance fact_finrank_euclideanSpace_fin_two : Fact (Module.finrank ℝ ℝ² = 2) :=
    38→  ⟨finrank_euclideanSpace_fin⟩

    40→open scoped EuclideanGeometry

    42→open scoped Real

    44→namespace EuclideanGeometry
    45→
    46→variable {V P : Type*} {n : ℕ}
    47→
    48→variable [NormedAddCommGroup V] [InnerProductSpace ℝ V] [MetricSpace P] [NormedAddTorsor V P]
    49→
    50→variable [Module.Oriented ℝ V (Fin 2)] [Fact (Module.finrank ℝ V = 2)] {p : Fin n → P}
    51→
    52→/-- We say a subset `A` of points in the plane is non-trilinear
    53→if it contains no three points that lie on the same line. -/
    54→def NonTrilinear (A : Set P) : Prop :=
    55→  A.Triplewise (fun x y z ↦ ¬ Collinear ℝ {x, y, z})
    56→
    57→/-- `ConvexIndep S` means that `S` consists of extremal points of its convex hull,
    58→i.e., the point set encloses a convex shape.
    59→Also known as a "convex-independent set". -/
    60→def ConvexIndep (S : Set ℝ²) : Prop :=
    61→  ∀ a ∈ S, a ∉ convexHull ℝ (S \ {a})
    62→
    63→/-- The set `P` contains a convex `n`-gon.
    64→See also `IsConvexPolygon`. -/
    65→def HasConvexNGon (n : ℕ) (P : Set ℝ²) : Prop :=
    66→  ∃ S : Finset ℝ², S.card = n ∧ ↑S ⊆ P ∧ ConvexIndep S
    67→
    68→/-- The statement that a sequence of points form a counter-clockwise convex polygon. -/
    69→def IsCcwConvexPolygon (p : Fin n → P) : Prop :=
    70→  ∀ ⦃i j k⦄, i < j → j < k → (∡ (p i) (p j) (p k)).sign = 1
    71→
    72→theorem IsCcwConvexPolygon.sign_oangle (hp : IsCcwConvexPolygon p) {i j k : Fin n}
    73→  (hij : i < j) (hjk : j < k) : (∡ (p i) (p j) (p k)).sign = 1 := hp hij hjk
    74→
    75→set_option linter.docPrime false in
    76→theorem IsCcwConvexPolygon.sign_oangle' (hp : IsCcwConvexPolygon p) {i j k : Fin n}
    77→    (hij : i < j) (hjk : j < k) : (∡ (p j) (p k) (p i)).sign = 1 := by
    78→  rw [EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rotate_sign]
    79→  exact hp hij hjk
    80→
    81→set_option linter.docPrime false in
    82→theorem IsCcwConvexPolygon.sign_oangle'' (hp : IsCcwConvexPolygon p) {i j k : Fin n}
    83→    (hij : i < j) (hjk : j < k) : (∡ (p k) (p i) (p j)).sign = 1 := by
    84→  rw [← EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rotate_sign]
    85→  exact hp hij hjk
    86→
    87→theorem IsCcwConvexPolygon.sign_oangle_finRotate (hp : IsCcwConvexPolygon p)
    88→    (hn : 3 ≤ n) (i : Fin n) :
    89→    (∡ (p i) (p <| finRotate _ i) (p <| finRotate _ (finRotate _ i))).sign = 1 := by
    90→  obtain ⟨n, rfl⟩ := le_iff_exists_add'.mp hn
    91→  by_cases hi : i = Fin.last (n + 2)
    92→  · rw [hi, finRotate_last, finRotate_apply_zero]
    93→    exact hp.sign_oangle'' Fin.zero_lt_one Fin.one_lt_last
    94→  by_cases hi' : finRotate _ i = Fin.last (n + 2)
    95→  · rw [hi', finRotate_last]
    96→    refine hp.sign_oangle' ?_ ((Fin.le_last _).lt_of_ne hi)
    97→    rw [Fin.pos_iff_ne_zero]
    98→    rintro rfl
    99→    rw [finRotate_apply_zero] at hi'
   100→    exact Fin.one_lt_last.ne hi'
   101→  apply hp.sign_oangle <;> apply lt_finRotate_of_ne_last <;> assumption
   102→
   103→@[simp] theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_zero (p : Fin 0 → P) : IsCcwConvexPolygon p := finZeroElim
   104→
   105→@[simp] theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_one (p : Fin 1 → P) : IsCcwConvexPolygon p := by intro; omega
   106→
   107→@[simp] theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_two (p : Fin 2 → P) : IsCcwConvexPolygon p := by intro; omega
   108→
   109→set_option linter.docPrime false in
   110→theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_four' {p : Fin 4 → P} :
   111→    IsCcwConvexPolygon p ↔ (∡ (p 0) (p 1) (p 2)).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ (p 1) (p 2) (p 3)).sign = 1 ∧
   112→    (∡ (p 2) (p 3) (p 0)).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ (p 3) (p 0) (p 1)).sign = 1 := by
   113→  refine ⟨fun h ↦ ?_, fun ⟨h1, h2, h3, h4⟩ ↦ ?_⟩
   114→  · obtain ⟨h01, h12, h23⟩ : (0 : Fin 4) < 1 ∧ (1 : Fin 4) < 2 ∧ (2 : Fin 4) < 3 := by simp
   115→    · repeat' constructor
   116→      · exact h.sign_oangle h01 h12
   117→      · exact h.sign_oangle h12 h23
   118→      · exact h.sign_oangle' (h01.trans h12) h23
   119→      · exact h.sign_oangle'' h01 (h12.trans h23)
   120→  · intro i j k hij hjk
   121→    fin_cases i <;> fin_cases j <;> fin_cases k <;> simp at hij hjk
   122→    · exact h1
   123→    · rw [EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rotate_sign]
   124→      exact h4
   125→    · rw [← EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rotate_sign]
   126→      exact h3
   127→    · exact h2

   129→@[simp]
   130→theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_four (A B C D : P) :
   131→    IsCcwConvexPolygon ![A, B, C, D] ↔
   132→      (∡ A B C).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ B C D).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ C D A).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ D A B).sign = 1 :=
   133→  isCcwConvexPolygon_four'
   134→
   135→/-- The statement that a sequence of points form a convex polygon. -/
   136→def IsConvexPolygon {n : ℕ} (p : Fin n → P) : Prop :=
   137→  IsCcwConvexPolygon p ∨ IsCcwConvexPolygon fun i => p (-i)
   138→
   139→/-- Three affine independent points always form a convex polygon. -/
   140→theorem isConvexPolygon_three_of_affineIndependent {A B C : P}
   141→    (hABC : AffineIndependent ℝ ![A, B, C]) : IsConvexPolygon ![A, B, C] := by
   142→  rw [← oangle_ne_zero_and_ne_pi_iff_affineIndependent, ← Real.Angle.sign_ne_zero_iff] at hABC
   143→  cases hsABC : (∡ A B C).sign
   144→  · exact (hABC hsABC).elim
   145→  · right
   146→    intro i j k hij hjk
   147→    fin_cases i <;> fin_cases j <;> fin_cases k <;> simp at hij hjk
   148→    rw [EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rev, Real.Angle.sign_neg, neg_eq_iff_eq_neg]
   149→    exact (oangle_rotate_sign A B C).trans hsABC
   150→  · left
   151→    intro i j k hij hjk
   152→    fin_cases i <;> fin_cases j <;> fin_cases k <;> simp at hij hjk
   153→    exact hsABC
   154→
   155→theorem isConvexPolygon_three_iff_affineIndependent {A B C : P} :
   156→    IsConvexPolygon ![A, B, C] ↔ AffineIndependent ℝ ![A, B, C] := by
   157→  refine ⟨fun h => ?_, isConvexPolygon_three_of_affineIndependent⟩
   158→  rw [← oangle_ne_zero_and_ne_pi_iff_affineIndependent, ← Real.Angle.sign_ne_zero_iff]
   159→  let p := ![A, B, C]
   160→  change IsConvexPolygon p at h
   161→  change Real.Angle.sign (∡ (p 0) (p 1) (p 2)) ≠ 0
   162→  cases h with
   163→  | inl h =>

   165→    rintro ⟨⟩
   166→  | inr h =>

   174→
   175→noncomputable def triangle_area (a b c : P) : ℝ :=
   176→  positiveOrientation.areaForm (a -ᵥ c) (b -ᵥ c) / 2
   177→
   178→lemma triangle_area_eq_det (a b c : ℝ²) :
   179→    triangle_area a b c =
   180→    Matrix.det !![a 0, b 0, c 0;
   181→                  a 1, b 1, c 1;
   182→                  1,   1,   1] / 2 := by
   183→  rw [triangle_area, Orientation.areaForm_to_volumeForm,
   184→    positiveOrientation.volumeForm_robust (EuclideanSpace.basisFun (Fin 2) ℝ) rfl, Module.Basis.det_apply]
   185→  suffices (a 0 - c 0) * (b 1 - c 1) - (b 0 - c 0) * (a 1 - c 1) =
   186→      a 0 * b 1 - a 0 * c 1 - b 0 * a 1 + b 0 * c 1 + c 0 * a 1 - c 0 * b 1 by
   187→    simp [Matrix.det_fin_two, Matrix.det_fin_three, Module.Basis.toMatrix, this]
   188→  ring
   189→
   190→/--
   191→Given a finite set of points in the plane, we define the number of distinct distances between pairs
   192→of points.
   193→-/
   194→noncomputable def distinctDistances (points : Finset ℝ²) : ℕ :=
   195→  (points.offDiag.image fun (pair : ℝ² × ℝ²) => dist pair.1 pair.2).card
   196→
   197→end EuclideanGeometry
   198→
   199→
   200→def IsIsosceles {α : Type*} [Dist α] (p q r : α) : Prop :=
   201→  dist p q = dist q r ∨ dist q r = dist r p ∨ dist r p = dist p q
   202→
   203→nonrec def Set.IsIsosceles {α : Type} [Dist α] (A : Set α) :=
   204→  Nonempty A ∧ A.Triplewise (IsIsosceles · · ·)
   205→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 662
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/662](https://www.erdosproblems.com/662)
    23→
    24→Consider the triangular lattice with minimal distance between two points $1$.
    25→Denote by $f(t)$ the number of lattice points within distance $t$ of any fixed
    26→lattice point. For example $f(1) = 6$, $f(\sqrt{3}) = 12$.
    27→
    28→Let $x_1, \ldots, x_n \in \mathbb{R}^2$ be such that $d(x_i, x_j) \geq 1$ for
    29→all $i \neq j$. Is it true that, provided $n$ is sufficiently large depending on
    30→$t$, the number of ordered pairs $(i, j)$ with $i \neq j$ and $d(x_i, x_j) \leq t$
    31→is at most $n \cdot f(t)$?
    32→
    33→Note: The original problem statement in [Er97e] contains apparent typos and is
    34→acknowledged as ambiguous. This formalization captures what appears to be the
    35→most natural interpretation: among unit-separated point sets, the triangular
    36→lattice maximizes the number of close pairs.
    37→
    38→A problem of Erdős, Lovász, and Vesztergombi.
    39→-/
    40→
    41→open Classical
    42→
    43→namespace Erdos662
    44→
    45→/-- $f(t)$ is the number of integer pairs $(a, b) \neq (0, 0)$ with
    46→$a^2 + ab + b^2 \leq t^2$, which equals the number of neighbors within distance
    47→$t$ of any point in the triangular lattice (with unit minimal distance). The
    48→squared distance from the origin to lattice point $(a, b)$ in the triangular
    49→lattice is $a^2 + ab + b^2$. -/
    50→noncomputable def triangularLatticeNeighborCount (t : ℝ) : ℕ :=
    51→  Set.ncard {p : ℤ × ℤ | p ≠ (0, 0) ∧
    52→    ((p.1 : ℝ) ^ 2 + (p.1 : ℝ) * (p.2 : ℝ) + (p.2 : ℝ) ^ 2) ≤ t ^ 2}
    53→
    54→/-- The number of ordered pairs of distinct points in $P$ with distance at most $t$. -/
    55→noncomputable def closePairCount
    56→    (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) (t : ℝ) : ℕ :=
    57→  ((P ×ˢ P).filter (fun pq => pq.1 ≠ pq.2 ∧ dist pq.1 pq.2 ≤ t)).card
    58→
    59→/--
    60→**Erdős Problem 662** [Er97e, p.532]:
    61→
    62→Among $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with minimum pairwise distance $\geq 1$, is
    63→the number of ordered pairs at distance $\leq t$ at most $n \cdot f(t)$, where
    64→$f(t)$ is the number of neighbors within distance $t$ in the triangular lattice?
    65→-/
    66→@[category research open, AMS 52]
    67→theorem erdos_662 : answer(sorry) ↔
    68→    ∀ t : ℝ, t > 0 →
    69→      ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N →
    70→        ∀ P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    71→          P.card = n →
    72→          (∀ p ∈ P, ∀ q ∈ P, p ≠ q → dist p q ≥ 1) →
    73→          closePairCount P t ≤ n * triangularLatticeNeighborCount t := by
    74→  sorry
    75→
    76→end Erdos662
    77→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 528
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/528](https://www.erdosproblems.com/528)
    23→
    24→Let $f(n,k)$ count the number of self-avoiding walks of $n$ steps beginning at the origin
    25→in the integer lattice $\mathbb{Z}^k$. The connective constant $C_k$ is defined as
    26→$C_k = \lim_{n \to \infty} f(n,k)^{1/n}$. The problem asks to determine $C_k$ exactly.
    27→
    28→[HM54] Hammersley, J. M. and Morton, K. W., *Poor man's Monte Carlo*. J. Roy. Statist.
    29→Soc. Ser. B (1954), 23–38.
    30→
    31→[Ke63] Kesten, H., *On the number of self-avoiding walks*. J. Math. Phys. (1963), 960–969.
    32→-/
    33→
    34→open Classical Finset BigOperators Filter
    35→
    36→namespace Erdos528
    37→
    38→/-- Two points in $\mathbb{Z}^k$ are adjacent in the integer lattice if their $\ell^1$ distance
    39→is $1$ (i.e., they differ by $\pm 1$ in exactly one coordinate). -/
    40→def LatticeAdj {k : ℕ} (x y : Fin k → ℤ) : Prop :=
    41→  (∑ i : Fin k, |x i - y i|) = 1
    42→
    43→/-- The number of self-avoiding walks of $n$ steps in $\mathbb{Z}^k$ starting at the origin.
    44→A self-avoiding walk is a path in the integer lattice $\mathbb{Z}^k$ that starts at the
    45→origin, takes $n$ steps along lattice edges, and never revisits a vertex. -/
    46→noncomputable def selfAvoidingWalkCount (n k : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    47→  Set.ncard {w : Fin (n + 1) → (Fin k → ℤ) |
    48→    w 0 = 0 ∧
    49→    (∀ i : Fin n, LatticeAdj (w ⟨i.val, by omega⟩) (w ⟨i.val + 1, by omega⟩)) ∧
    50→    Function.Injective w}
    51→
    52→/-- The connective constant $C_k$ for self-avoiding walks on $\mathbb{Z}^k$, defined as
    53→$C_k = \lim_{n \to \infty} f(n,k)^{1/n}$ where $f(n,k)$ is the number of self-avoiding
    54→walks of $n$ steps. Returns the limit value if it exists, or $0$ otherwise. -/
    55→noncomputable def connectiveConstant (k : ℕ) : ℝ :=
    56→  if h : ∃ C : ℝ, Filter.Tendsto
    57→    (fun n : ℕ => ((selfAvoidingWalkCount n k : ℝ)) ^ ((1 : ℝ) / (n : ℝ)))
    58→    atTop (nhds C)
    59→  then h.choose
    60→  else 0
    61→
    62→/--
    63→Erdős Problem 528 asks to determine the connective constant $C_k$ exactly, where $C_k$ is
    64→the limit of $f(n,k)^{1/n}$ as $n \to \infty$ and $f(n,k)$ counts the number of
    65→self-avoiding walks of $n$ steps in $\mathbb{Z}^k$.
    66→
    67→Hammersley and Morton [HM54] showed this limit exists. Kesten [Ke63] proved
    68→$C_k = 2k - 1 - 1/(2k) + O(1/k^2)$.
    69→-/
    70→@[category research open, AMS 5 82]
    71→theorem erdos_528 :
    72→    ∀ k : ℕ, 0 < k →
    73→    connectiveConstant k = answer(sorry) := by
    74→  sorry
    75→
    76→/-- Variant: The connective constant $C_k$ exists and satisfies the known bounds
    77→$k \le C_k \le 2k - 1$ [HM54] [Ke63]. -/
    78→@[category research solved, AMS 5 82]
    79→theorem erdos_528.variants.existence_with_bounds :
    80→    ∀ k : ℕ, 0 < k →
    81→    ∃ C : ℝ,
    82→      (k : ℝ) ≤ C ∧ C ≤ 2 * (k : ℝ) - 1 ∧
    83→      Filter.Tendsto
    84→        (fun n : ℕ => ((selfAvoidingWalkCount n k : ℝ)) ^ ((1 : ℝ) / (n : ℝ)))
    85→        atTop (nhds C) := by
    86→  sorry
    87→
    88→end Erdos528
    89→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 529
    21→
    22→Let $d_k(n)$ denote the expected Euclidean distance from the origin of the endpoint of a
    23→uniformly random $n$-step self-avoiding walk on $\mathbb{Z}^k$. The problem asks whether
    24→$d_2(n)/\sqrt{n} \to \infty$ (Part 1) and whether $d_k(n) \ll \sqrt{n}$ for $k \geq 3$ (Part 2).
    25→
    26→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/529](https://www.erdosproblems.com/529)
    27→
    28→[DuHa13] Duminil-Copin, H. and Hammond, A., *Self-avoiding walk is sub-ballistic*,
    29→J. Eur. Math. Soc. (2013).
    30→-/
    31→
    32→open BigOperators Filter Classical
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos529
    35→
    36→/-- Two points in $\mathbb{Z}^k$ are adjacent in the integer lattice if their $\ell^1$ distance
    37→is $1$ (i.e., they differ by $\pm 1$ in exactly one coordinate). -/
    38→def LatticeAdj {k : ℕ} (x y : Fin k → ℤ) : Prop :=
    39→  (∑ i : Fin k, |x i - y i|) = 1
    40→
    41→/-- The set of self-avoiding walks of $n$ steps in $\mathbb{Z}^k$ starting at the origin. -/
    42→def selfAvoidingWalks (n k : ℕ) : Set (Fin (n + 1) → (Fin k → ℤ)) :=
    43→  {w | w 0 = 0 ∧
    44→    (∀ i : Fin n, LatticeAdj (w ⟨i.val, by omega⟩) (w ⟨i.val + 1, by omega⟩)) ∧
    45→    Function.Injective w}
    46→
    47→/-- The Euclidean distance of a point in $\mathbb{Z}^k$ from the origin. -/
    48→noncomputable def euclidDistFromOrigin {k : ℕ} (x : Fin k → ℤ) : ℝ :=
    49→  Real.sqrt (∑ i : Fin k, ((x i : ℝ)) ^ 2)
    50→
    51→/-- The expected Euclidean distance from the origin of the endpoint of a uniformly
    52→random self-avoiding walk of $n$ steps in $\mathbb{Z}^k$.
    53→$$d_k(n) = \frac{1}{|\mathrm{SAW}(n,k)|} \cdot \sum_{w \in \mathrm{SAW}(n,k)} \|w(n)\|_2$$ -/
    54→noncomputable def expectedSAWDist (n k : ℕ) : ℝ :=
    55→  if h : (selfAvoidingWalks n k).Finite then
    56→    (h.toFinset.sum (fun w => euclidDistFromOrigin (w ⟨n, by omega⟩))) /
    57→    (h.toFinset.card : ℝ)
    58→  else 0
    59→
    60→/--
    61→Erdős Problem 529, Part 1:
    62→
    63→Let $d_k(n)$ be the expected distance from the origin after taking $n$ steps of a
    64→uniformly random self-avoiding walk in $\mathbb{Z}^k$. Is it true that
    65→$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{d_2(n)}{n^{1/2}} = \infty?$$
    66→
    67→That is, in two dimensions, does the expected endpoint distance grow strictly
    68→faster than $\sqrt{n}$?
    69→
    70→Duminil-Copin and Hammond [DuHa13] proved $d_2(n) = o(n)$. It is conjectured
    71→that $d_2(n) \sim D \cdot n^{3/4}$.
    72→-/
    73→@[category research open, AMS 5 60]
    74→theorem erdos_529 : answer(sorry) ↔
    75→    Tendsto (fun n : ℕ => expectedSAWDist n 2 / (n : ℝ) ^ ((1 : ℝ) / 2))
    76→      atTop atTop := by
    77→  sorry
    78→
    79→/--
    80→Erdős Problem 529, Part 2:
    81→
    82→Is it true that $d_k(n) \ll n^{1/2}$ for $k \geq 3$?
    83→
    84→Hara and Slade proved $d_k(n) \sim D \cdot n^{1/2}$ for all $k \geq 5$. It is now believed
    85→that this is false for $k = 3$ and $k = 4$: the conjectured behavior is
    86→$d_3(n) \sim n^{\nu}$ where $\nu \approx 0.59$ and
    87→$d_4(n) \sim D \cdot (\log n)^{1/8} \cdot n^{1/2}$.
    88→-/
    89→@[category research open, AMS 5 60]
    90→theorem erdos_529.variants.part2 : answer(sorry) ↔
    91→    ∀ k : ℕ, k ≥ 3 →
    92→    ∃ C : ℝ, 0 < C ∧ ∀ᶠ n in atTop,
    93→      expectedSAWDist n k ≤ C * (n : ℝ) ^ ((1 : ℝ) / 2) := by
    94→  sorry
    95→
    96→end Erdos529

    23→
    24→For $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ we define the upper density as
    25→$$\bar{\delta}(A) = \limsup_{R\to\infty} \frac{\lambda(A \cap B_R)}{\lambda(B_R)},$$
    26→where $\lambda$ is the Lebesgue measure and $B_R$ is the ball of radius $R$.
    27→
    28→Estimate $m_1 = \sup \bar{\delta}(A)$, where $A$ ranges over all measurable subsets of
    29→$\mathbb{R}^2$ without two points distance $1$ apart. In particular, is $m_1 \le 1/4$?
    30→
    31→Originally a question of Moser [Mo66]. The trivial upper bound is $m_1 \le 1/2$,
    32→since for any unit vector $u$ the sets $A$ and $A + u$ must be disjoint.
    33→A lower bound of $m_1 \ge \pi/(8\sqrt{3}) \approx 0.2267$ comes from the union of open
    34→circular discs of radius $1/2$ at a regular hexagonal lattice.
    35→
    36→Proved by Ambrus, Csiszárik, Matolcsi, Varga, and Zsámboki [ACMVZ23],
    37→who showed $m_1 \le 0.247 < 1/4$.
    38→
    39→[Mo66] Moser, L., *Problem 10*, Canad. Math. Bull. 9 (1966).
    40→
    41→[Er85] Erdős, P., *Problems and results in combinatorial geometry*, 1985, p. 4.
    42→
    43→[ACMVZ23] Ambrus, G., Csiszárik, A., Matolcsi, M., Varga, D., and Zsámboki, P.,
    44→*The density of planar sets avoiding unit distances*, 2023.
    45→-/
    46→
    47→open MeasureTheory Metric
    48→
    49→namespace Erdos232
    50→
    51→/-- A set in a metric space is *unit-distance-free* if no two points in the set
    52→are exactly distance $1$ apart. -/
    53→def IsUnitDistanceFree {X : Type*} [PseudoMetricSpace X] (A : Set X) : Prop :=
    54→  ∀ x ∈ A, ∀ y ∈ A, dist x y ≠ 1
    55→
    56→/--
    57→Erdős Problem 232 [Er85, p.4] (PROVED):
    58→
    59→For every measurable unit-distance-free set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$, the upper density
    60→$$\limsup_{R\to\infty} \frac{\lambda(A \cap B_R)}{\lambda(B_R)} \le \frac{1}{4}.$$
    61→
    62→Formalized as: for every $\varepsilon > 0$, for all sufficiently large $R$, the density
    63→ratio $\lambda(A \cap B_R) / \lambda(B_R) \le 1/4 + \varepsilon$. This is equivalent to the
    64→$\limsup$ being at most $1/4$.
    65→
    66→Proved by Ambrus–Csiszárik–Matolcsi–Varga–Zsámboki [ACMVZ23] who showed
    67→the stronger bound $m_1 \le 0.247$.
    68→-/
    69→@[category research solved, AMS 28 52]
    70→theorem erdos_232 : answer(True) ↔
    71→    ∀ (A : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))),
    72→      MeasurableSet A → IsUnitDistanceFree A →
    73→      ∀ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε →
    74→        ∃ R₀ : ℝ, 0 < R₀ ∧ ∀ R : ℝ, R₀ ≤ R →
    75→          (volume (A ∩ Metric.ball (0 : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) R)).toReal /
    76→          (volume (Metric.ball (0 : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) R)).toReal ≤ 1 / 4 + ε := by
    77→  sorry
    78→
    79→end Erdos232
    80→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 211
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/211](https://www.erdosproblems.com/211)
    23→
    24→Let $1 \leq k < n$. Given $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, at most $n - k$ on any line,
    25→there are $\gg kn$ many lines which contain at least two points.
    26→
    27→Proved by Beck [Be83] and Szemerédi–Trotter [SzTr83].
    28→
    29→In particular, given any $2n$ points with at most $n$ on a line there
    30→are $\gg n^2$ many lines formed by the points.
    31→
    32→[Be83] Beck, J., _On the lattice property of the plane and some problems of Dirac,
    33→Motzkin and Erdős in combinatorial geometry_. Combinatorica 3 (1983), 281-297.
    34→
    35→[SzTr83] Szemerédi, E. and Trotter, W.T., _Extremal problems in discrete geometry_.
    36→Combinatorica 3 (1983), 381-392.
    37→-/
    38→
    39→open Classical
    40→
    41→namespace Erdos211
    42→
    43→/-- Points in the Euclidean plane $\mathbb{R}^2$. -/
    44→abbrev Point2 := EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)
    45→
    46→/-- Point $r$ lies on the line through points $p$ and $q$. -/
    47→def LiesOnLine (p q r : Point2) : Prop :=
    48→  ∃ t : ℝ, r - p = t • (q - p)
    49→
    50→/-- The line through two points, as a set of points. -/
    51→def lineThrough (p q : Point2) : Set Point2 :=
    52→  {r | LiesOnLine p q r}
    53→
    54→/-- The number of points in $S$ on the line through $p$ and $q$. -/
    55→noncomputable def pointsOnLine (S : Finset Point2) (p q : Point2) : ℕ :=
    56→  (S.filter (fun r => LiesOnLine p q r)).card
    57→
    58→/-- The number of distinct lines containing at least two points of $S$. -/
    59→noncomputable def lineCount (S : Finset Point2) : ℕ :=
    60→  (((S ×ˢ S).filter (fun pq => pq.1 ≠ pq.2)).image
    61→    (fun pq => lineThrough pq.1 pq.2)).card
    62→
    63→/--
    64→Erdős Problem 211 (Proved by Beck [Be83] and Szemerédi–Trotter [SzTr83]):
    65→
    66→There exists an absolute constant $C > 0$ such that for any finite set $S$
    67→of points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with $|S| = n$, if $1 \leq k < n$ and at most $n - k$ points
    68→of $S$ lie on any single line, then the number of distinct lines containing
    69→at least two points of $S$ is at least $C \cdot k \cdot n$.
    70→-/
    71→@[category research solved, AMS 5 52]
    72→theorem erdos_211 :
    73→    ∃ C : ℝ, C > 0 ∧
    74→      ∀ (S : Finset Point2) (k : ℕ),
    75→        1 ≤ k →
    76→        k < S.card →
    77→        (∀ p ∈ S, ∀ q ∈ S, p ≠ q → pointsOnLine S p q ≤ S.card - k) →
    78→        (lineCount S : ℝ) ≥ C * ↑k * ↑S.card := by
    79→  sorry
    80→
    81→end Erdos211
    82→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 215
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/215](https://www.erdosproblems.com/215)
    23→
    24→Does there exist a set $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ such that every set congruent to $S$
    25→contains exactly one point of $\mathbb{Z}^2$? The answer is yes, proved by Jackson and
    26→Mauldin [JaMa02] using the axiom of choice.
    27→
    28→[JaMa02] Jackson, S. and Mauldin, R.D., _On a lattice problem of H. Steinhaus_, Journal of the
    29→American Mathematical Society 15 (2002), 817-856.
    30→-/
    31→
    32→namespace Erdos215
    33→
    34→/--
    35→The integer lattice $\mathbb{Z}^2 \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$: points whose coordinates are all
    36→integers.
    37→-/
    38→def IntLattice : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) :=
    39→  {p | ∀ i : Fin 2, ∃ n : ℤ, p i = ↑n}
    40→
    41→/--
    42→Erdős Problem 215 (Steinhaus Problem):
    43→Does there exist $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ such that every set congruent to $S$
    44→(that is, $S$ after some isometry) contains exactly one point from $\mathbb{Z}^2$?
    45→
    46→The answer is yes, proved by Jackson and Mauldin [JaMa02].
    47→Their construction depends on the axiom of choice.
    48→-/
    49→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
    50→theorem erdos_215 : answer(True) ↔
    51→    ∃ (S : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))),
    52→      ∀ (f : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2) → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    53→        Isometry f →
    54→        ∃! p, p ∈ f '' S ∧ p ∈ IntLattice := by
    55→  sorry
    56→
    57→end Erdos215

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 659
    21→
    22→*References:*
    23→- [erdosproblems.com/659](https://www.erdosproblems.com/659)
    24→- [MoOs06] Moree, Pieter and Osburn, Robert, Two-dimensional lattices with few distances. Enseign. Math. (2) (2006), 361--380
    25→- [ErFi96] Erdős, Paul and Fishburn, Peter, Maximum planar sets that determine {$k$} distances. Discrete Math. (1996), 115--125.
    26→- [Gr26](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.09102): Benjamin Grayzel, Solution to a Problem of Erdős Concerning Distances and Points
    27→-/
    28→
    29→open EuclideanGeometry Finset Real
    30→
    31→namespace Erdos659
    32→
    33→/--
    34→Is there a set of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ such that every subset of $4$ points determines at
    35→least $3$ distances, yet the total number of distinct distances is $\ll \frac{n}{\sqrt{\log n}}$?
    36→
    37→There does exist such a set: a suitable truncation of the lattice
    38→$\{(a,b\sqrt{2}): a,b\in\mathbb{Z}\}$ suffices. This construction appears to have been first
    39→considered by Moree and Osburn \cite{MoOs06}, who proved that it has
    40→ $\ll \frac{n}{\sqrt{\log n}}$ many distinct distances. This construction was independently found by
    41→ [Lund and Sheffer](https://adamsheffer.wordpress.com/2014/07/16/point-sets-with-few-distinct-distances/),
    42→ who further noted that this configuration contains no squares or equilateral triangles.
    43→
    44→There are only six possible configurations of $4$ points which determine only $2$ distances
    45→(first noted by Erdős and Fishburn [ErFi96]), and five of them contain either a square or an
    46→equilateral triangle. The remaining configuration contains four points from a regular pentagon,
    47→and Grayzel [Gr26] (using Gemini) has noted in the comments that this configuration can also be
    48→ruled out, thus giving a complete solution to this problem. Boris Alexeev using Aristotle provides
    49→a formalisation of the proof.
    50→-/
    51→@[category research formally solved using lean4 at "https://github.com/plby/lean-proofs/blob/226d5fad7143dcebea2bbb5ec87f18a3a1dcea69/src/v4.24.0/ErdosProblems/Erdos659.lean", AMS 52]
    52→theorem erdos_659 : answer(True) ↔ ∃ A : ℕ → Finset ℝ²,
    53→   (∀ n, #(A n) = n ∧ ∀ S ⊆ A n, #S = 4 → 3 ≤ distinctDistances S) ∧
    54→    (fun n ↦ distinctDistances (A n)) ≪ fun n ↦ n / sqrt (log n) := by
    55→  sorry
    56→
    57→end Erdos659
    58→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 93
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/93](https://www.erdosproblems.com/93)
    23→
    24→If $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are in convex position (vertices of a convex polygon), then they
    25→determine at least $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ distinct distances. Proved by Altman.
    26→
    27→[Al63] Altman, E., _On a problem of P. Erdős_. Amer. Math. Monthly 70 (1963), 148-157.
    28→-/
    29→
    30→open scoped Classical
    31→
    32→namespace Erdos93
    33→
    34→/--
    35→A finite set of points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ is in convex position if no point lies in the
    36→convex hull of the remaining points. Equivalently, the points are the vertices
    37→of a convex polygon.
    38→-/
    39→def InConvexPosition (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    40→  ∀ p ∈ A, p ∉ convexHull ℝ ((↑A : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) \ {p})
    41→
    42→/--
    43→The set of distinct pairwise distances between distinct points in a finite
    44→point set in $\mathbb{R}^2$.
    45→-/
    46→noncomputable def distinctDistances (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Finset ℝ :=
    47→  A.offDiag.image (fun pq => dist pq.1 pq.2)
    48→
    49→/--
    50→Erdős Problem 93: If $n$ distinct points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ form a convex polygon, then they
    51→determine at least $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ distinct distances. Proved by Altman [Al63].
    52→-/
    53→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
    54→theorem erdos_93
    55→    (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)))
    56→    (hconv : InConvexPosition A)
    57→    (hn : 2 ≤ A.card) :
    58→    A.card / 2 ≤ (distinctDistances A).card := by
    59→  sorry
    60→
    61→end Erdos93

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 94
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/94](https://www.erdosproblems.com/94)
    23→
    24→For $n$ points in convex position with distance multiplicities $f(u_i)$, the sum
    25→$\sum_i f(u_i)^2$ is $O(n^3)$. Proved by Lefmann and Thiele.
    26→
    27→[LeTh95] Lefmann, H. and Thiele, T., _Point sets with distinct distances_.
    28→Combinatorica 15 (1995), 379–408.
    29→-/
    30→
    31→namespace Erdos94
    32→
    33→/--
    34→A finite set of points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ is in convex position if no point lies in
    35→the convex hull of the remaining points. Equivalently, all points are
    36→vertices of their convex hull (they form a convex polygon).
    37→-/
    38→def ConvexPosition (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    39→  ∀ p ∈ A, p ∉ convexHull ℝ ((A : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) \ {p})
    40→
    41→/--
    42→The count of ordered quadruples $(a, b, c, d)$ from $A$ with $a \neq b$, $c \neq d$,
    43→and $\operatorname{dist}(a, b) = \operatorname{dist}(c, d)$. This equals
    44→$4 \cdot \sum_i f(u_i)^2$ where $f(u_i)$ counts unordered pairs at distance $u_i$.
    45→-/
    46→noncomputable def distSqSum (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : ℕ :=
    47→  let E := EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)
    48→  Set.ncard {x : E × E × E × E |
    49→    x.1 ∈ (A : Set E) ∧ x.2.1 ∈ (A : Set E) ∧
    50→    x.2.2.1 ∈ (A : Set E) ∧ x.2.2.2 ∈ (A : Set E) ∧
    51→    x.1 ≠ x.2.1 ∧ x.2.2.1 ≠ x.2.2.2 ∧
    52→    dist x.1 x.2.1 = dist x.2.2.1 x.2.2.2}
    53→
    54→/--
    55→Suppose $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are in convex position (they form the vertices of
    56→a convex polygon). Let $\{u_1, \ldots, u_t\}$ be the set of distinct pairwise
    57→distances, and let $f(u_i)$ denote the number of pairs at distance $u_i$. Then
    58→$$\sum_i f(u_i)^2 \ll n^3.$$
    59→
    60→This was proved by Lefmann and Thiele [LeTh95] under the weaker
    61→assumption that no three points are collinear.
    62→-/
    63→@[category research solved, AMS 5 52]
    64→theorem erdos_94 :
    65→    ∃ C : ℝ, C > 0 ∧
    66→    ∀ A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    67→      ConvexPosition A →
    68→      (distSqSum A : ℝ) ≤ C * (A.card : ℝ) ^ 3 := by
    69→  sorry
    70→
    71→end Erdos94
    72→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 96
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/96](https://www.erdosproblems.com/96)
    23→
    24→Erdős–Moser conjecture: if $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are in convex position, then the number
    25→of pairs at unit distance is $O(n)$.
    26→-/
    27→
    28→namespace Erdos96
    29→
    30→/--
    31→A finite set of points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ is in convex position if no point lies in the
    32→convex hull of the remaining points. Equivalently, the points are the vertices
    33→of a convex polygon.
    34→-/
    35→def ConvexPosition (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    36→  ∀ p ∈ P, p ∉ convexHull ℝ (↑(P.erase p) : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)))
    37→
    38→/--
    39→The number of ordered pairs of distinct points in $P$ that are at unit distance.
    40→(Using ordered pairs; the number of unordered pairs is exactly half this,
    41→so the $O(n)$ bound is equivalent.)
    42→-/
    43→noncomputable def unitDistancePairCount (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : ℕ :=
    44→  (P.offDiag.filter (fun pq => dist pq.1 pq.2 = 1)).card
    45→
    46→/--
    47→Erdős Problem 96 (Erdős–Moser conjecture):
    48→If $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ form a convex polygon (are in convex position), then there

    52→set $P$ of points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ in convex position, the number of (ordered) pairs at
    53→unit distance is at most $C \cdot |P|$.
    54→-/
    55→@[category research open, AMS 52]

    57→    ∃ C : ℝ, C > 0 ∧
    58→    ∀ (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))),
    59→      ConvexPosition P →
    60→      (unitDistancePairCount P : ℝ) ≤ C * (P.card : ℝ) := by

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 10
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/10](https://www.erdosproblems.com/10)
    23→-/
    24→
    25→namespace Erdos10
    26→
    27→/--
    28→The set of natural numbers that can be written as a sum
    29→of a prime and at most $k$ powers of $2$.
    30→-/
    31→abbrev sumPrimeAndTwoPows (k : ℕ) : Set ℕ :=
    32→  { p + (pows.map (2 ^ ·)).sum | (p : ℕ) (pows : Multiset ℕ) (_ : p.Prime)
    33→    (_ : pows.card ≤ k)}
    34→
    35→/--
    36→Is there some $k$ such that every integer is the sum of a prime and at most $k$
    37→powers of $2$?
    38→-/
    39→@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
    40→theorem erdos_10 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∃ k, sumPrimeAndTwoPows k = Set.univ \ {0, 1} := by
    41→  sorry
    42→
    43→/--
    44→Gallagher [Ga75] has shown that for any $ϵ > 0$ there exists $k(ϵ)$
    45→such that the set of integers which are the sum of a prime and at most $k(ϵ)$
    46→many powers of $2$ has lower density at least $1 - ϵ$.
    47→
    48→Ref: Gallagher, P. X., _Primes and powers of 2_.
    49→-/
    50→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
    51→theorem erdos_10.variants.gallagher (ε : ℝ)
    52→    (hε : 0 < ε) : ∃ k, 1 - ε ≤ (sumPrimeAndTwoPows k).lowerDensity := by
    53→  sorry
    54→
    55→/--
    56→Granville and Soundararajan [GrSo98] have conjectured that at most $3$
    57→powers of $2$ suffice for all odd integers, and hence at most $4$ powers of $2$
    58→suffice for all even integers.
    59→
    60→Ref: Granville, A. and Soundararajan, K., _A Binary Additive Problem of Erdős and the Order of $2$ mod $p^2$_
    61→-/
    62→@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
    63→theorem erdos_10.variants.granville_soundararajan_odd :
    64→    {n : ℕ | Odd n ∧ 1 < n} ⊆ sumPrimeAndTwoPows 3 ∧
    65→      {n : ℕ | Even n ∧ n ≠ 0} ⊆ sumPrimeAndTwoPows 4 := by
    66→  sorry
    67→
    68→/--
    69→Bogdan Grechuk has observed that `1117175146` is not the sum of a prime
    70→and at most $3$ powers of $2$.
    71→-/
    72→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
    73→theorem erdos_10.variants.grechuk_example :
    74→    1117175146 ∉ sumPrimeAndTwoPows 3 := by
    75→  sorry
    76→
    77→/--
    78→There are infinitely many even integers not the sum of a prime and $2$ powers of $2$
    79→-/
    80→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
    81→theorem erdos_10.variants.two_pows :
    82→    Set.Infinite <| {n : ℕ | Even n} \ sumPrimeAndTwoPows 2 := by
    83→  sorry
    84→
    85→/--
    86→Bogdan Grechuk has observed that $1117175146$ is not the sum of a prime and at most $3$
    87→powers of $2$, and pointed out that parity considerations, coupled with the fact that there
    88→are many integers not the sum of a prime and $2$ powers of $2$ suggest that there exist
    89→infinitely many even integers which are not the sum of a prime and at most $3$ powers of $2$).
    90→-/
    91→@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
    92→theorem erdos_10.variants.grechuk :
    93→    Set.Infinite <| {n : ℕ | Even n} \ sumPrimeAndTwoPows 3 := by
    94→  sorry
    95→
    96→end Erdos10

## Summary of Search Results

I've conducted a thorough search of the FormalConjectures directory for code related to convex sets, convex bodies, lattice points, integer points, volume, area, and Pick's theorem. Here are my findings:

#### **FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/** Directory

Contains 1179 Erdos problem formalization files. Key lattice/convex-related problems:

**Lattice Point & Lattice-Related Problems:**
